summary: The main claim is: with pseudocode) how to create the Intrinsic Cost module.
I argue that APTAMI is in fact, at least possibly, a path towards that latter destination, but only if we can solve a hard and currently-unsolved technical problem.

top_comment.author: tailcalled
top_comment.text:
Nice, I was actually just thinking that someone needed to respond to LeCun's proposal.

That said, I think you may have gotten some of the details wrong. I don't think the intrinsic cost module gets raw sensory data as input, but instead it gets input from the latent variables of the world model as well as the self-supervised perception module. This complicates some of the safety problems you suggest.
top_comment.claim: The counterclaim is: That said, I think you may have gotten some of the details wrong.

crux.crux_question: On module, does the disagreement hinge on problem (post) versus input (comment)?
crux.evidence_post: On a more technical level, we face (among other things) the classic problem of out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, and we face it not once but twice: Out-of-distribution generalization problem 1: How does the Prosociality Score Model generalize from the supervised (human-labeled) examples to the AI’s future perceptions—which might be far outside that training distribution? Indeed, it is an open technical problem to write down any Intrinsic Cost function (along with training environment and other design choices) for which there is a strong reason to believe that the resulting AI would be controllable and/or motivated by human welfare , while also being sufficiently competent to do the hard intellectual tasks that we’re hoping for (e.g.
crux.evidence_comment: I don't think the intrinsic cost module gets raw sensory data as input, but instead it gets input from the latent variables of the world model as well as the self-supervised perception module.

summary: The main claim is: After fine-tuning with RAFT, stable diffusion is able to generate good results.
The following figure shows the results before and after fine-tuning with RAFT.
The main claim is: Alignment with our brain values is often secondary to alignment with our body values.
This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.

The main claim is: Successful control proposals will only mitigate AGI-induced existential risks—the putative goal of AGI safety research—if they are actually used, which means that it is incumbent on AGI safety researchers to devote serious effort and atten…
Our goal is to figure out technical solutions to the AGI safety control problem.
The main claim is: Compute performance on AI workloads should increase with transistor scaling.
Related scaling trends are mostly also following transistor density.
The main claim is: If you’ve read about alignment research and you want to start contributing, the new iteration of the AI Safety Camp is a great opportunity!
If you care about alignment and are in this situation, I encourage you to apply for a project without required skillsets and learn what you need as you go along.
The main claim is: There are hidden assumptions in this model which should be made explicit so that their probability can be assessed.
The main claim is: Let Lt1 be an optimizer with objective function L(t), and let Lt2 be an optimizer with objective function G(t).
Let C(L1) be the capability of optimizer Lt1, and let α(L1,L2) be the alignment of optimizer Lt2 with optimizer Lt1.
